title: Countries by income classification
subtitle: The World Bank's income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.
note: Countries are grouped based on the income classification for each respective year. This means that group membership can change over time.
caption:
Countries by income classification

The World Bank's income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.

This stacked area chart displays the number of countries in four income classifications from 1987 to 2024. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 250, representing the count of countries, while the horizontal axis marks years in five-year intervals. Four colored bands represent the income groups: low income (purple) at the bottom, lower-middle income (pink) above it, upper-middle income (light green) in the middle, and high income (dark green) at the top. In 1987, the low-income band starts near 50 countries and rises to a peak around 1995 before declining steadily to approximately 25 by 2024. The lower-middle income band begins near 50, expands to roughly 65 in the mid-1990s, then contracts to about 50 by the end of the period. The upper-middle income band starts around 30 countries, grows to nearly 40 by 1995, and remains relatively stable through 2024. The high-income band begins near 40 countries, increases sharply to over 60 by 1990, and continues growing to approximately 85 by 2024, forming the largest single group. The total number of countries represented rises from about 165 in 1987 to over 215 by 2024. Countries are grouped based on the income classification for each respective year, meaning group membership can change over time as nations move between categories.
